docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: API Integration Test
docs.text: This page verifies that the Docs-as-Code pipeline is fully functional. If you can see this, the CI/CD trigger successfully built the latest commit.

docs.title: Technical Writing Samples
docs.text: !!! info "Documentation Standard" We are using Material for MkDocs. This allows us to use high-visibility callouts (admonitions) to separate technical data from coaching advice.

docs.title: The 'H' in Webhooks: A Phonetic Guide
docs.text: In technical coaching, pronunciation and clarity are just as vital as the code itself. "Webhooks" is often a stumbling block for non-native speakers, yet it is a cornerstone of modern event-driven architecture.

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text: Clear documentation isn't just about what you write; it's about how accessible that information is to a global audience.

docs.title: API vocabulary essentials
docs.text: This article explores the most important terms used in API design and development.

docs.title: 1. API
docs.text: An API, or Application Programming Interface, is a set of rules that lets one software system communicate with another.

docs.title: 2. Endpoint
docs.text: An endpoint is a specific URL where an API exposes a resource or action, such as /users or /orders/42.

docs.title: 3. Resource
docs.text: A resource is the thing an API manages, like a user, product, invoice, or post.

docs.title: 4. Request
docs.text: A request is the message a client sends to an API to ask for data or trigger an action.

docs.title: 5. Response
docs.text: A response is the message the API sends back after processing a request.

docs.title: 6. HTTP method
docs.text: An HTTP method describes the action the client wants to perform, such as GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE.

docs.title: 7. Status code
docs.text: A status code is the numeric result of a request, such as 200 for success, 201 for created, or 404 for not found.

docs.title: 8. Parameter
docs.text: A parameter adds input to an API call. Common types include path parameters, query parameters, and header parameters.

docs.title: 9. Payload
docs.text: A payload is the data sent in a request body or returned in a response body, often in JSON format.

docs.title: 10. Authentication
docs.text: Authentication is the process of verifying who a client or user is, often using an API key, token, or OAuth flow.

docs.title: 11. Authorization
docs.text: Authorization determines what an authenticated user is allowed to do, such as reading data but not deleting it.

docs.title: 12. Rate limiting
docs.text: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make in a given time period to prevent abuse and keep the API stable.

docs.title: 13. Versioning
docs.text: Versioning is the practice of managing changes to an API over time, often by using paths like /v1/ and /v2/.

docs.title: 14. Schema
docs.text: A schema defines the structure and rules for data, including required fields, data types, and formats.

docs.title: 15. Pagination
docs.text: Pagination splits large result sets into smaller pages so responses stay fast and manageable.

docs.title: 16. Sorting
docs.text: Sorting lets clients control the order of returned data, such as by name, date, or price.

docs.title: 17. Filtering
docs.text: Filtering narrows results based on criteria, such as only showing active users or published posts.

docs.title: 18. Serialization
docs.text: Serialization converts data into a format that can be sent over the network, such as JSON.

docs.title: 19. Deserialization
docs.text: Deserialization converts received data back into a usable object or structure in code.

docs.title: 20. Documentation
docs.text: Documentation explains how to use the API, including endpoints, request formats, examples, and error handling.

docs.title: Formatting notes
docs.text: For MkDocs Material, this page works well with clear headings, short paragraphs, and simple Markdown structure. You can also add callouts, code blocks, and tables later if you want to expand the article.
